working-tree-staging-and-history.label: Move a Change into History
working-tree-staging-and-history.summary: Follow one change through the working tree, staging area, and committed history without introducing later inspection commands early.

sketch0.title: The Working Tree Is the Draft

sketch1.title: The Staging Area Is the Proposal

try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.title: Read a Working-Tree Change
try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.prompt: You are working in the prepared `study-notes/` repository. `notes.txt` has one unstaged edit. Use short status to locate that change in the working-tree column.
try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.hint: The file changed after the last snapshot and has not been staged.

help.concept: An unstaged tracked-file edit appears in the working-tree column.
help.hint_1: The path already has history, so it is not untracked.
help.hint_2: Use short status and read the second column beside `notes.txt`.

try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.starterCode:
Git tracks snapshots.
The working tree can change first.
try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.instructions: You are working in the prepared `study-notes/` repository. `notes.txt` has one unstaged edit. Use short status to locate that change in the working-tree column.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf study-notes
mkdir -p study-notes
git -C study-notes init -q -b main
git -C study-notes config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C study-notes config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
cd study-notes
printf %s 'Git tracks snapshots.
' > notes.txt
cd ..
git -C study-notes add -A
git -C study-notes commit -q -m 'Add project baseline'
cd study-notes
printf %s 'Git tracks snapshots.
The working tree can change first.
' > notes.txt
cd ..

git -C study-notes commit -q -m 'Add project baseline'

printf %s 'Git tracks snapshots.
The working tree can change first.
' > notes.txt
cd ..

try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.title: Move the Change into the Staging Area
try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.prompt: You are working in the prepared `study-notes/` repository. Stage only `notes.txt`, then use short status to confirm that the change moved into the staging column.
try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.hint: Select the named file, then check which status column contains the mark.

help.concept: Staging changes the proposed snapshot, not the working file.
help.hint_2: After staging, short status should place the modification on the left.

try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.starterCode:
Git tracks snapshots.
The staging area selects the next snapshot.
try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.instructions: You are working in the prepared `study-notes/` repository. Stage only `notes.txt`, then use short status to confirm that the change moved into the staging column.

try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.title: Record the Staged Snapshot
try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.prompt: You are working in the prepared `study-notes/` repository. The notes change is staged. Record it with the message `Explain the staging area`, then use short status to confirm that no work remains.
try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.hint: Record the proposed snapshot, then inspect the current state.

help.concept: A commit records the staged version and leaves other current-state work outside.
help.hint_1: Use the exact project result named in the task as the subject.
help.hint_2: A quiet short-status result confirms that the three layers agree.

try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.starterCode:
Git tracks snapshots.
The staging area selects the next snapshot.
try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.solutionCode:
git commit -m "Explain the staging area"
git status --short
try_working_tree_staging_and_history_ske.instructions: You are working in the prepared `study-notes/` repository. The notes change is staged. Record it with the message `Explain the staging area`, then use short status to confirm that no work remains.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf study-notes
mkdir -p study-notes
git -C study-notes init -q -b main
git -C study-notes config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C study-notes config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
cd study-notes
printf %s 'Git tracks snapshots.
' > notes.txt
cd ..
git -C study-notes add -A
git -C study-notes commit -q -m 'Add project baseline'
cd study-notes
printf %s 'Git tracks snapshots.
The staging area selects the next snapshot.
' > notes.txt
cd ..
git -C study-notes add -- notes.txt

git -C study-notes add -- notes.txt

single-choice-1.title: Identify the Working Tree
single-choice-1.prompt: What is the working tree in Git?
single-choice-1.hint: Focus on what the command or concept observes or changes at this point in the workflow.

help.concept: Follow one edit from the working tree to the staging area and into a named commit, checking each boundary along the way.
help.hint_1: Remove options that act on a different repository layer than the one named in the question.
help.hint_2: Choose the answer that remains accurate even when other unrelated files exist.

options.a: The directory where you make changes to files
options.b: The area where changes are staged for commit

single-choice-2.title: Purpose of the Staging Area
single-choice-2.prompt: What is the purpose of the staging area in Git?
single-choice-2.hint: Focus on what the command or concept observes or changes at this point in the workflow.

help.concept: Follow one edit from the working tree to the staging area and into a named commit, checking each boundary along the way.
help.hint_1: Remove options that act on a different repository layer than the one named in the question.
help.hint_2: Choose the answer that remains accurate even when other unrelated files exist.

options.a: To prepare changes for the next commit

multi-choice-1.title: What a Commit Records
multi-choice-1.prompt: Which facts about a commit are correct? Select all that apply.
multi-choice-1.hint: Judge each option independently; choose only the statements that stay true in this exact repository state.

help.concept: Follow one edit from the working tree to the staging area and into a named commit, checking each boundary along the way.
help.hint_1: A partly true option is still wrong if it adds an effect Git does not guarantee.
help.hint_2: Use the layer named in the prompt—working tree, staging area, or history—to test each choice.

options.b: It includes a message and author identity.
options.c: It automatically includes every unstaged edit.
options.d: It becomes part of repository history.

drag-reorder-1.title: Order of Git Workflow
drag-reorder-1.prompt: Arrange the steps of the Git workflow in the correct order.
drag-reorder-1.hint: Start with the state that must exist before the next action can make sense.

help.concept: Follow one edit from the working tree to the staging area and into a named commit, checking each boundary along the way.
help.hint_1: Track how the project moves from working files to a proposed snapshot and then to history.
help.hint_2: Read the ordered steps as one continuous repository story.

tokens.t1: Edit files in the working tree
tokens.t2: Stage changes in the staging area
tokens.t3: Commit changes to the commit history

working-tree-intro.title: The Working Tree Is the Draft
working-tree-intro.bodyMarkdown:
The working tree is the project you can open and edit now. When a tracked file changes, short status places the mark in the right column because the edit exists only in the working copy.

History does not move automatically. Git waits for you to decide whether the change belongs in the next snapshot.

staging-area-intro.title: The Staging Area Is the Proposal
staging-area-intro.bodyMarkdown:
The staging area is the version you are proposing for the next commit. Select a path with:

```bash
git add notes.txt
```

Afterward, short status moves the mark to the left column. `git add` is taught here once; later work will reuse it to build focused snapshots.

commit-history-intro.title: A Commit Records the Proposal
commit-history-intro.bodyMarkdown:
A commit records exactly what is staged, together with an author and a message:

```bash
git commit -m "Explain the staging area"
```

Untracked paths and unstaged edits remain outside. Check short status afterward: a quiet result means the working tree and staging area agree with the new commit. This is the one introduction to `git commit`; later lessons will focus on better boundaries and messages.
